scenarios.task: Our women's outerwear buy planned a 35% sell-through by week 4. We're at 22% actual at the 4-week checkpoint. A colleague says let's wait and see how week 8 looks before doing anything, since markdowns hurt margin. What's your call?
criteria: Recommends taking an early markdown or pausing reorders now rather than waiting for the 8-week checkpoint
States that deferring action converts a manageable early markdown into a much larger, more damaging clearance problem later
Quantifies or acknowledges the specific shortfall (22% vs 35% planned, 13 points below plan) as a clear trigger, not a borderline case
Pushes back on the 'wait and see' framing given the size of the shortfall at this early checkpoint
Does not endorse deferring the decision to the 8-week checkpoint

scenarios.task: A buy just closed the season with a 48% realized margin, well above our typical 40% target. The buyer wants to call it a clear win and repeat the same open-to-buy approach next season. GMROI came in at 1.85 against our department's 2.5 benchmark. Good call to repeat it?
criteria: Flags that GMROI (1.85), not margin percentage (48%), is the real measure of the buy's capital efficiency, and it's below the 2.5 benchmark
States that a strong margin percentage can still represent a weak use of capital if the inventory turned slowly
Pushes back on labeling the buy a clear win purely based on the margin percentage
Recommends investigating why GMROI underperformed (e.g. slow sell-through requiring markdowns) before deciding to repeat the same approach next season
Does not endorse simply repeating the same open-to-buy approach without addressing the GMROI shortfall

scenarios.task: We're finalizing a vendor deal for next season. The vendor offered a decent unit cost and we're ready to sign. Separately, we're planning assortment for a new athleisure line that mixes wardrobe basics (leggings, tees) with a trend collection (a specific print/colorway drop). Anything to flag before finalizing either?
criteria: Recommends negotiating cash discount percentage and dating/payment terms as part of the same conversation as unit cost, not settling for unit cost alone
States that negotiating only unit price leaves real, quantifiable margin on the table

Recommends treating the wardrobe basics as core/replenishable items (ongoing reorder based on sell-through) and the trend print/colorway as fashion/trend items (defined markdown cadence, no reorder assumption)
Warns against applying a single unified strategy (either reorder-based or markdown-cadence-based) across both categories
Addresses both the vendor terms gap and the assortment classification issue, not just one
